summary:
The Kids Internet and Digital Safety Act (KIDS Act) establishes a federal framework to protect minors online by imposing new requirements on certain websites, apps, online video games, and artificial intelligence chatbots. The bill defines a minor as an individual under 17 years old.

Title I requires online platforms where more than one-third of the material is sexual content harmful to minors to use commercially available age-verification technology to block minors from accessing that material. It prohibits the retention or transfer of verification data beyond what is strictly necessary and does not require government-issued identification. This title preempts state laws on the same subject.

Title II applies to platforms that enable user-generated content sharing, use design features to promote engagement, and use personal information for advertising or recommendations. It requires these platforms to implement policies addressing harms such as threats of violence, sexual exploitation, and promotion of narcotics or gambling to minors. Platforms must provide minors with safeguards to limit communications, control recommendation systems, restrict geolocation sharing, and reduce compulsive usage features, with the most protective settings enabled by default. Parental tools must allow parents to manage privacy settings, restrict purchases, and view time spent. Platforms must offer reporting mechanisms, disclose advertising labels, and prohibit advertising illegal products to known minors. Annual third-party audits are required, with public reporting of certain metrics. This title also bans ephemeral messaging features for all minors and direct messaging for children under 13, while requiring parental controls for direct messaging for teens. It prohibits market or product-focused research on minors except for safety, security, or legal compliance purposes. State laws conflicting with these provisions are preempted.

Title III requires online video game providers to offer parents safeguards to limit communications, restrict purchases, and manage screen time for minor users, with the most protective settings as the default.

Title IV requires chatbot providers to disclose that a chatbot is an artificial intelligence system, provide crisis hotline resources when prompted about suicide, and implement policies to address access to harmful material and promote breaks after three hours of continuous use. Chatbots are prohibited from falsely claiming to be licensed professionals.

Title V directs the Federal Trade Commission and other agencies to conduct studies on social media use, fentanyl access, and chatbot mental health effects, and to develop public education resources. It also establishes a Kids Internet Safety Partnership to identify best practices.

The bill generally takes effect one year after enactment, with specific provisions taking effect earlier. Enforcement is carried out by the Federal Trade Commission and state attorneys general, with violations treated as unfair or deceptive acts. The bill includes rules of construction stating it does not require weakening encryption, collecting additional age data, or disclosing private communications.
